coredata.dc:title: Design and fabrication of carbon quantum dots decorated NiCo2S4 for high stability supercapacitors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fengying
Yan, Mengyuan
Liu, Huichen
Nai, Jihai
Liu, Guijing
coredata.dc:description: Transition metal sulfides (such as nickel cobalt sulfide) are one of the ideal choices for active materials for supercapacitors with excellent electrical conductivity, low electronegativity, and low cost. In this research, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are introduced by the hydrothermal approach to boost the specific capacitance (Cs) and cycling stability of nickel cobalt sulfide (NiCo2S4). Synergistic interaction between CQDs and NiCo2S4 provides more active sites and enables fast electron transfer. Additionally, the cooperative effect of both enhances the NiCo2S4@CQDs-x structural cohesion, which maintains stability and avoids structural collapse during long-cycle. The NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material exhibits high Cs (1005.56 F/g at 1 A/g) and good rate performance (92.86 % at 10 A/g). Additionally, the assembled NiCo2S4@CQDs//AC supercapacitor demonstrates a power density (PD) of 801.99 W/kg at an energy density (ED) of 35.78 Wh/kg and good long-cycle durability with 95.83 % capacity retention under 10 A/g for 10,000 cycles. NiCo2S4@CQDs-based supercapacitors are expected to provide efficient and stable energy solutions for portable electronic devices, renewable energy systems, and electric vehicles, thereby advancing the development of sustainable energy technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
NiCo2S4
Hydrothermal process
Asymmetric supercapacitors
Long-term cyclic stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of NiCo2S4 and NiCo2S4@CQDs composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR (B) UV–vis of CQDs-x; (C) UV–vis absorption and PL spectrum of CQDs-180; (D) PL spectra of CQDs-180 under various excitation wavelengths.
SEM images of (A-B) NiCo2S4 and (C–D) NiCo2S4@CQDs-180; (E-F4) TEM images of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180.
(A) XPS spectra of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180; High-resolution XPS spectra of (B) Ni 2p, (C) Co 2p, (D) S 2p, (E) C 1s and (F) N 1s.

(A) CV curves of various samples at 10 mV/s; (B) GCD curves of various samples at 1 A/g; (C) Specific capacities at different current density 1–40 A/g; (D) CV curves of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 (2–100 mV/s); (E) Power-law dependence of anodic and cathodic peak current (log(i)) under various scan rates (log(v)); (F) The contribution of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 at 20 mV/s; (G) The calculated contribution of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 (2–100 mV/s); (H) EIS plots; (I) 10,000 cycle stability tests at 20 A/g.
Schematic diagram of macro and micro transmission of OH− and e− in the NiCo2S4@CQDs.

(A) Schematic of ACS apparatus; CV curves of ASC (B) at different voltage Windows and (C) at various scan rates; (D) GCD curve of ASC; (E) Ragone plot of ACS with other works for comparison; (F) 10,000 cycle stability tests at 10 A/g.

head.ce:title: Design and fabrication of carbon quantum dots decorated NiCo2S4 for high stability supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Ludong University, Yantai 264025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transition metal sulfides (such as nickel cobalt sulfide) are one of the ideal choices for active materials for supercapacitors with excellent electrical conductivity, low electronegativity, and low cost. In this research, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are introduced by the hydrothermal approach to boost the specific capacitance (Cs) and cycling stability of nickel cobalt sulfide (NiCo2S4). Synergistic interaction between CQDs and NiCo2S4 provides more active sites and enables fast electron transfer. Additionally, the cooperative effect of both enhances the NiCo2S4@CQDs-x structural cohesion, which maintains stability and avoids structural collapse during long-cycle. The NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material exhibits high Cs (1005.56 F/g at 1 A/g) and good rate performance (92.86 % at 10 A/g). Additionally, the assembled NiCo2S4@CQDs//AC supercapacitor demonstrates a power density (PD) of 801.99 W/kg at an energy density (ED) of 35.78 Wh/kg and good long-cycle durability with 95.83 % capacity retention under 10 A/g for 10,000 cycles. NiCo2S4@CQDs-based supercapacitors are expected to provide efficient and stable energy solutions for portable electronic devices, renewable energy systems, and electric vehicles, thereby advancing the development of sustainable energy technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In today's technological landscape that's evolving rapidly, advancements in energy storage technology have become crucial to supporting a wide range of high-tech applications [1,2]. Owing to their significant PD, quick charge-discharge capabilities, and superior cycle life, supercapacitors have gained substantial attention [3–5]. In comparison with traditional capacitors, supercapacitors also offer wide operating range, low cost, safety etc. They are broadly used in numerous applications that need high power and extended cycle life, like electric vehicles, power generation, grid stabilization, and renewable energy systems [6–8]. Despite their many benefits, there are still many challenges to further improve their Cs, ED, and cycle durability. To tackle these issues, investigators have been actively delving into and developing innovative electrode materials. Among these, NiCo2S4 has shown great promise when utilized as electrode materials in supercapacitors. NiCo2S4 has received widespread notice due to its excellent conductivity, relatively low price, and low electronegativity [9]. Compared to single-component nickel or cobalt compounds, nickel-cobalt bimetallic compounds exhibit higher electrochemical activity [5,10]. NiCo2S4 offers even greater electrochemical activity than nickel-cobalt bimetallic compounds [11]. However, NiCo2S4 still encounters some practical limitations, such as poor cycle stability and insufficient specific surface area, which constrains its further application in supercapacitors. To overcome these issues, researchers have employed various strategies to improve NiCo2S4-based electrode materials. One common strategy is to add carbon-derived materials to boost the comprehensive properties of the electrodes [12]. At present, graphene and carbon nanotubes stand out as the two most studied carbon materials in the report, although they are capable of improving the long-term cycle durability of NiCo2S4, the high cost seriously hinders practical applications [11,13,14].

CQDs, a newly emerging category of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have become a focal point of extensive scientific investigation in contemporary materials research. Their appeal stems from a combination of advantageous properties, including large specific surface area, cost-effectiveness and non-toxicity. CQDs possess plentiful surface functional groups, which boost their versatility and utility in various applications. As a result of these characteristics, CQDs have become an important material for improving the properties of energy storage devices [15,16]. In addition, CQDs have excellent photovoltaic properties and fast electron transfer capabilities, which have been widely used in the fields of biosensing, catalysis and optoelectronic devices [17,18]. CQDs have also been widely used to synthesize transition metal sulfide composites to further enhance their electrochemical performance [19,20]. Luo et al. verified the ability of CQDs to enhance the structural integrity of NiCo-LDH nano-arrays and efficiently avoid the volume change-induced collapse in long-term cycles [21]. So, the development of carbon materials with simple preparation process and low cost is expected to solve the problem of poor cycle stability of NiCo2S4 in supercapacitors.

In this study, CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal reaction using low-cost urea and citric acid as raw materials. Subsequently, the prepared CQDs were combined with NiCo2S4 by hydrothermal method aimed to making full use of the unique properties of the two substances. The results demonstrate that the incorporation of CQDs improves the structural durability and boosts the Cs of the composites. The NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 exhibits large specific surface area and elevates charge-transfer efficiency, which significantly enhances capacitance and rate performance. This composite has great potential for application in supercapacitors. The NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material achieves a high Cs of up to 1005.56 F/g at 1 A/g. Moreover, it demonstrates good rate performance with 92.86 % at 10 A/g. Additionally, the NiCo2S4@CQDs//AC hybrid supercapacitor has a high PD of 801.99 W/kg and long cycle durability of 95.83 % (10,000 cycles at 10 A/g). Through addressing current challenges and exploiting the unique advantages of CQDs and NiCo2S4, new ideas and directions are being provided for the evolution of more efficient and longer-lasting energy storage.
Materials, material preparation and materials characterization can be found in the Supplementary Information.

Every electrochemical evaluation was performed in an electrochemical workstation system. The preparation method of the electrodes can be found in the Supporting Information. The electrochemical characterization of the electrodes had been studied in a three-electrode system. In this system, Pt plate served as the counter electrode, Hg/HgO was utilized as the reference electrode, and 3 M KOH solution acted as the electrolyte. All the active material mass of the electrode ranged from 0.0010 to 0.0014 g.

The calculation of the Cs (F/g) from the GCD curves is carried out as follows:
(1)
C =
I ∆ t
m ∆ V
In this equation, I (A) signifies the discharge current, Δt (s) denotes the discharge time, m (g) indicates the mass of the active material, and ΔV (V) denotes the potential window.

To ensure charge equilibrium between the cathode and anode electrodes in asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) testing, the mass of active material on each electrode is determined through the eq. Q+ = Q− as follows:
(2)
M +
M −
=
C × Δ
V −
C × Δ
V +
In this equation, M (g) represents the mass loading, C (F/g) stands for the Cs.

Additionally, the Cs of the NiCo2S4//AC and NiCo2S4@CQDs//AC device was computed based on Eq. (1), where the mass was the sum of the active materials from both electrodes. The ED (E, Wh/kg) and PD (P, W/kg) of the ASC are calculated through these equations [22]:
(3)
E =
C s
× Δ
V 2
7.2
(4)
P =
3600 × E
∆ t

The chemical composition of CQDs was investigated using FT-IR spectra. In Fig. 1A, CQDs-180 and CQDs-200 have the most peaks, indicating that CQDs have more functional group kinds at these two temperatures. This is because the higher temperature promotes full carbonation of precursor and the generation of more nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups. The increase in these functional groups helps to offer more active sites and improve the electron/ion transport efficiency, thereby boosting the electrochemical properties of the material. CQDs-140 and CQDs-160 have fewer peaks because their precursors do not carbonize and carbonize incompletely [23]. The wide peak observed around 3340 cm−1 is the stretching vibrational absorption of OH. Stretching vibrational peaks of CO and CC in the carbonyl groups of amides, carboxylic acids, or ketones are observed at 1630 and 1360 cm−1 [24,25], which contribute to the fluorescent nature of CQDs [26]. The stretching vibration peaks at 1250 and 1110 cm−1 are the C-O(s) and C-O(b) peaks [24]. The CN and NH stretching vibration peaks appear at 1460 and 1580 cm−1 [27,28]. The presence of functional groups such as -NH2, -COOH and -OH on CQDs is demonstrated by FT-IR spectra [26,29].

The optical characteristics of CQDs were further studied with Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra. In Fig. 1B–C, the π-π* transition associated with the aromatic CC bond occurs at 235 nm [30,31]. The broad peak at 344 nm is caused by the n-π* transition related to the CO bond [31,32]. Notably, CQDs synthesized at 180 °C exhibit the strongest and most pronounced absorption peaks owing to the fact that the lack of energy at lower temperatures inhibits the conversion of -OH, which affects the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs [33]. Too high a temperature leads to excessive carbonization and agglomeration of CQDs, which weakens the fluorescence properties of CQDs [34,35]. It is shown that the temperature significantly affects the formation of functional groups and optical properties. When excited at 390 nm, CQDs-180 produces an intense peak at 448 nm (Fig. 1C). This demonstrates that the CQDs have the ability to re-emit absorbed light as fluorescence [36]. In addition, the PL emission behavior of CQDs-180 under various excitation wavelengths was also investigated (Fig. 1D). PL peaks show a significant redshift when excitation wavelengths exceed 400 nm, suggesting that the PL emission is excitation-dependent. This excitation-dependent behavior is due to the abundance of functional groups in CQDs, demonstrating its multicolor properties [35]. The -COOH and -C=O on the CQDs surface are able to form coordination bonds with the metal ions in NiCo2S4, which are beneficial for CQDs to be tightly bonded with NiCo2S4 surface or embedded in the pore structure. This effectively increases the contact area of the charge transfer pathway and optimises electrochemical properties of the composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of NiCo2S4 and NiCo2S4@CQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The process for preparing NiCo2S4@CQDs-x is illustrated in Fig. 2 . The prepared CQDs-x solution is transferred into an autoclave along with a homogeneous solution that contains Co2+, Ni2+ and C2H5NS. Then, the mixture is treated hydrothermally at 180 °C for 24 h. After washing and drying, the composite material in granular form is obtained as the SC electrode material. NiCo2S4 and CQDs are linked by hydrogen bonds, coordination bonds and van der Waals forces. These interactions can boost the utilisation efficiency of electrochemical active sites by optimizing the material's electron transport pathways and ion diffusion process. Meanwhile, these interactions make CQDs more closely bound to NiCo2S4, which is conducive to the material's structural stability and cycle life.

The morphology of materials is one of the main factors that determine their electrochemical properties. SEM images of NiCo2S4 (Fig. 3A–B) show a highly aggregated granular structure with relatively larger particle sizes. In contrast, the SEM images of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 (Fig. 3C–D) show uniform particle distribution, small volume and compact structure. This results from the interface interaction between the CQDs and NiCo2S4, thus making the composite's structure more compact. NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 is composed of particles according to TEM results, which agrees with SEM results. In addition, a clear gap between the particles can be observed (Fig. 3E-F4). Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis confirms crystal structure of material. Fig. S1 and Fig. 3F show that the CQDs are monodisperse. The 0.24, 0.28 and 0.33 nm lattice fringes exhibited in the HRTEM images belong to the (400), (311), and (220) crystal planes of the NiCo2S4. The phase and crystal structure of NiCo2S4 and NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 material were further studied using XRD. The XRD patterns of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 display major diffraction peaks corresponding to NiCo2S4 and standard card (Fig. S2). This shows that the crystal structure of material has not changed during the synthesis of CQDs and NiCo2S4. These above results illustrate the successful composite of NiCo2S4 and CQDs.

To quantify these morphological changes, the adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore diameter distribution curves of NiCo2S4 and NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 samples have been analyzed by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) theory. As shown in Fig. S3A–B, the Langmuir type IV isotherms of both samples show hysteresis loop in the 0.7–1 relative pressure range, indicating mesoporous nature (2–50 nm) in the tested materials. The presence of mesopores is further evidenced by the sharp increase in adsorption at lower relative pressures [22,37]. The mesoporous structure of the material facilitates faster diffusion of electrons and ions to the electrodes [37]. The specific surface area of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 (61.65 m2/g) sample is threefold higher than that of NiCo2S4 (17.61 m2/g) sample. The pore volume of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 (0.28 cm3/g) is better than that of NiCo2S4 (0.11 cm3/g). The average pore diameter of the two samples has been determined by the Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method through desorption isotherm analysis. With the average pore sizes of the NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 and NiCo2S4 being 16.55 nm and 17.62 nm, the existence of mesopores is further confirmed. Additionally, broader pore size distribution observed in both samples indicates that the synthesized materials are porous. Compared with NiCo2S4 materials, NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 composites exhibit a larger specific surface area, which can enlarge the effective touching area on the electrode/electrolyte interface, thus offering multiple active sites for both charge storage and ion exchange [38–40]. The high pore volume and narrow pore size ensure movement and diffusion of electrolyte ions during rapid charging and discharging at high current densities [22]. The rise in specific surface area is mainly owing to the embedding of CQDs, which forms more pores between the particles. At the same time, many -NH2, -COOH and -OH that are present on the surface of CQDs can engage with NiCo2S4 particles and change their growth, thus effectively preventing the particles from agglomerating. This structural optimisation is useful for promoting the electrochemical performance of material.

The chemical bond between NiCo2S4 and CQDs was further verified by FTIR diagram of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 (Fig. S4). The wide peak at 3442 cm−1 arises from H2O's OH stretching vibration. These peaks at 1252, 1380 and 1630 cm−1 arises from COC, CO and CC, respectively [41,42]. These bonds indicate the presence of -OH and -COOH in CQDs, favoring synthesis of composites [43]. Ni/Co-O produces stretching vibration at 621 cm−1 [1,30]. The chemical bonds and elemental makeup of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 were further investigated through XPS analysis. The C, O, S, Ni, Co and N elements in the composite can be found in the full spectrum (Fig. 4A). The peaks at 853 eV and 870.4 eV are for Ni2+, while the peaks at 856.3 eV and 874.1 eV are Ni3+ (Fig. 4B). Fig. 4C displays that these peaks at 778.4 eV and 793.4 eV belong to Co2+, and these peaks of 780.9 eV and 796.9 eV belong to Co3+, respectively [43]. These peaks at 161.9 eV and 163.1 eV are related to the surface low-coordinated S2− bonds and metal‑sulfur bonds [44] (Fig. 4D). In Fig. 4E, these peaks at 285.5, 286.7, 284.5, 289.0 eV are attributed to CS, CN, CC and CO bonds. CN and CS bonds indicate successful doping of S and N elements into the CQDs [45]. Fig. 4F reveals that peaks at 400.1, 401.0 and 401.6 eV belong to pyridinic N, CoN and graphitic N [30,46]. These results collectively confirm the successful integration of CQDs into NiCo2S4.

Furthermore, the thermal stability of both materials was investigated. From the TGA curves (Fig. S5), the addition of CQDs enhances the thermal stability of the material. The curve has three mass loss stages. The drop at 0–170 °C is caused by evaporation of water molecules. The drop at 170–500 °C is due to sublimation of sulfur [47,48]. The drop at 500–900 °C corresponds to the breakup of NiSO4 and CoSO4 into stable NiO and CoO [43] and the further combustion of CQDs. The weight loss of NiCo2S4@CQDs is less than that of the pure NiCo2S4 material, indicating that the integration of CQDs notably enhances the thermal stability of the composite material.

The investigation into the electrochemical performance of NiCo2S4 and NiCo2S4@CQDs-x electrode materials was conducted. Fig. 5A shows CV curves at 10 mV/s in the range of 0–0.6 V. The area of the CV curves for the NiCo2S4@CQDs-x is remarkably larger than that of the pure NiCo2S4, denoting that NiCo2S4@CQDs-x possesses a higher Cs. Notably, the NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material exhibits the largest CV area and the highest Cs at 10 mV/s. These symmetric Faradaic redox peaks are owing to the highly reversible oxidation and reduction reactions of Ni3+/Ni2+ and Co4+/Co3+/Co2+, demonstrating Faradic capacitive behavior. The corresponding reaction equations are as follows [43]:
(5)
NiS +
OH −
↔ NiSOH +
e −
(6)
CoS +
OH −
↔ CoSOH +
e −
(7)
CoSOH +
OH −
↔ CoSO +
H 2
O +
e −

Fig. 5B presents the GCD curves at 1 A/g within 0–0.54 V. These GCD curves show nonlinear characteristics and the results are compatible with CV curves, further confirming the Faradaic redox characteristics. Among them, the NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material has the largest Cs, which shows that CQDs contribute to the improvement of electron transfer rate and capacitance. Additionally, temperature also has an effect on the material morphology (Fig. S6), where NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material has small particles and uniform distribution. This morphology promotes rapid entry of electrolyte ions and shortens the ion diffusion paths, thus contributing to superior energy storage capacity [49]. Furthermore, the injection of CQDs strengthens electrical conductivity and accelerates electron transfer [49]. In Fig. S7, the shape of GCD curve of all samples does not deform, signifying good reversibility and rate performance [30]. In Fig. 5C, the Cs of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material is 1005.56, 987.78, 980.56, 891.85 and 843.71 F/g at 1, 2, 3, 20 and 40 A/g, demonstrating its superiority over other materials. The elevated Cs results from the porous structure, many functional groups and the synergistic interaction between CQDs and NiCo2S4 [43,50]. Among all electrode materials, NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 has the best capacitance retention (92.86 %) at 10 A/g, 61.69 % retention for NiCo2S4, 70.61 % retention for NiCo2S4@CQDs-140, 81.59 % retention for NiCo2S4@CQDs-160 and 81.86 % retention for NiCo2S4@CQDs-200. The improvement in retention rate is due to the high conductivity of CQDs, which significantly boosts the electron transport rate. The extensive surface area of the electrode material offers a lot of active sites for ion diffusion within electrolyte. In addition, the charge and discharge curve times of all electrode materials are almost equal, indicating that they have good coulombic efficiency [51].

In Fig. 5D, these CV curves maintain a consistent and symmetrical shape at 75 and 100 mV/s, indicating that the electrode is electrochemically reversible [51]. Both the area of the CV curve and the peak current grow with increasing scan rate. The shifts of the oxidation peak to the right and the reduction peak to the left implies that electrode has good rate performance and efficient electron and ion transport ability [50]. With the aim of delving more profoundly into the energy storage mechanism of the NiCo2S4@CQDs-180, electrochemical dynamics were explored through CV tests at scan rates ranging from 2 to 100 mV/s. This current dependency on scan rate is expressed by the formula [52]:
(8)
i = a
v b

In this equation, i and v
are the peak current and scanning rates of the electrodes, respectively. The b value is the slope of the log
i
and log
v
curve.

At b = 1, the charge storage mechanism in the electrode material is capacitively controlled, while when b = 0.5, it is diffusively controlled. The peak values of 0.5171 and 0.71069 at the cathodic and anodic can be obtained from Fig. 5E, indicating that the NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material is dominated by diffusion-control. In addition, the capacitance control current (
k 1
v ) and diffusion control current (
k 2
v
1 / 2
) are expressed by the following equations [53]:
(9)
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
where
k 1
and
k 2
are the slopes of the linear fits of i/υ 1/2 and (υ)1/2.

Fig. 5F shows that the diffusion contribution dominates at the 20 mV/s scan rate. As the scanning rate rises (Fig. 5G), the contribution from diffusion steadily diminishes, while the capacitive contribution gradually increases. This phenomenon is elucidated by the fact that as the scanning speed increases, the pseudo-capacitance dominates; while diffusion is inhibited, the redox reaction is confined to surface, and the redox inside the material is inhibited. At low scanning speed, diffusion dominates capacity and the pseudo-capacitance dominates less. This shows that the Cs of the NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material is mainly due to the pseudocapacitive nature and partially due to the capacitive nature of the double layers [22].

Furthermore, in the high-frequency region NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material has a lower intercept with the X-axis than the other materials, suggesting a low internal resistance. In the low-frequency region NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material has the largest slope, suggesting faster electrolyte diffusion and ionic transport rates (Fig. 5H). Both EIS and GCD curves indicate that the NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material exhibits good ion transfer rate and excellent electrochemical performance. The integration of CQDs can accelerate the electron/ion migration, and the porous structure of the material can afford extensive channels for ion diffusion and conversion [30]. Furthermore, the durability of electrochemical cycling was assessed by performing 10,000 long-cycles at 20 A/g. Fig. 5I shows that the capacity retention rate of NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 electrode material is 83.33 % while that of pure NiCo2S4 is 59.57 %. In addition, the coulomb efficiency of the two materials is about 97.43 %. The poor cycling stability of NiCo2S4 is attributed to the repeated insertion and removal of alkaline electrolyte ions causing lattice disruption and S dissolution [54]. The enhanced cycling durability can mainly be ascribed to the synergistic influence of CQDs and NiCo2S4. Incorporating CQDs not only helps to stabilise the morphology of the electrode but also enhances its conductivity, thus benefiting the overall performance of the electrode [15,55]. Furthermore, loose material and porous structure can withstand more volume expansion and contraction [56]. The elevated electrochemical performance of the NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 is ascribed to the following reasons: (1) CQDs have good conductivity, which is favorable for the effective electron transport pathways in the composites and promotes the electrochemical reaction rate. (2) The abundant -COOH, -NH2 and -OH on the surface of CQDs add additional electrochemical active sites, which are favorable for the capacitance and energy storage capacity of the composites. (3) The porous structure of NiCo2S4@CQDs accelerates the ion diffusion within electrolyte and speeds up Faradaic reaction to realize high capacity and long cycle life [57,58].

The NiCo2S4@CQDs's structural design gives it good electrochemical properties. During the charging process (Fig. 6 ), electrons begin to move in reaction to the applied electric field and create a potential difference. Ions of the electrolyte accumulate over the boundary of the electrode material and form an electric double layer. As ions from the electrolyte continue to accumulate, they enter the lattice of the material and generate pseudocapacitance through redox reactions [59].

NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 exhibits the best electrochemical performance in all tests. NiCo2S4 and NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 were assembled into ASC devices to further validate their practical applications, where the NiCo2S4 and NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 worked as the cathodes, and AC (the mass of the AC was 0.0021 g) used as the anode electrode (Fig. 7A). They are respectively known as NiCo2S4//AC and NiCo2S4@CQDs//AC. Initially, the voltage window of CV curve for the ASC device was tested. Once the voltage window is increased to 1.8 V (Fig. 7B), these CV curves are distorted indicating polarization [57,60]. Consequently, the optimal voltage window is established to be 1.6 V. The comparison of the electrochemical performance between NiCo2S4//AC devices and NiCo2S4@CQDs//AC was conducted (Fig. S9A–B). The CV integral area of NiCo2S4@CQDs//AC devices are observed to be significantly larger than that of the original material, which indicates a higher capacity. The GCD curves also verify that the composite material's Cs is better than that of the original material. The subsequent studies emphasize the electrochemical performance of NiCo2S4@CQDs//AC. Fig. 7C shows that the CV curve shape changes very little as the scan rate increases, which indicates that ACS devices have commendable rate capability [49,50]. The GCD curves at different current densities (Fig. 7D) exhibit nearly symmetric triangular shapes, reflecting good electrochemical reversibility [30]. The Cs at 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10 A/g are 100.63, 94.75, 90.11, 87.25, 86.73, 85.20, 81.34, 80.11, 77.62 and 76.12 F/g (Fig. S10). The Ragone plot (Fig. 7E) illustrates that the ED reaches a maximum value of 35.78 Wh/Kg as the PD is 801.99 W/Kg. The ED decreases to 27.07 Wh/Kg and the PD reaches up to 8778.32 W/Kg. The performance of ACS devices is competitive with other previous materials [9,61–67]. Furthermore, cycle durability is a crucial element for assessing the practical applicability of ASCs. The long-cycle durability of the ASC was evaluated. Upon completing 10,000 cycles (Fig. 7F), the capacitance retention rates for the NiCo2S4//AC and NiCo2S4@CQDs//AC hybrid supercapacitor are 73.21 % and 95.83 % (cyclic stability is superior to most other reported literatures (Table S1)), while the coulomb efficiency remains at about 95.83 %, demonstrating remarkable durability. All results confirm the potential of NiCo2S4@CQDs as ACS energy storage device. Besides, the assembled device successfully lit the small bulb (Fig. S11), showing its possible application value as an energy storage device.

In this study, NiCo2S4@CQDs-x composites were successfully generated using hydrothermal approach. The incorporation of CQDs greatly increases the pore volume and specific surface area, thereby improving electron and ion transport. Moreover, the cooperative effect between CQDs and NiCo2S4 gave NiCo2S4@CQDs-180 excellent electrochemical properties. Notably, it had high Cs (1005.56 F/g at 1 A/g) and well rate performance (92.86 % capacitance retention at 10 A/g). In addition, the NiCo2S4@CQDs//AC hybrid supercapacitor had a PD of 801.99 W/kg under an ED of 35.78 Wh/kg and good long cycle durability (95.83 % capacity retention for 10,000 cycles at 10 A/g), showing its broad application prospect in energy storage equipment. The successful fabrication of NiCo2S4@CQDs solves the poor durability of NiCo2S4 in long-cycle and offers new insights and methods for the advancement of high-performance energy storage materials.

Fengying Wang: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mengyuan Yan: Conceptualization. Huichen Liu: Methodology. Jihai Nai: Software. Guijing Liu: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022QB089).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2025.116543.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Room temperature synthesis of vertically aligned amorphous ultrathin NiCo-LDH nanosheets bifunctional flexible supercapacitor electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Owusu, K. A.; Wang, Z.; Saad, A.; Boakye, F. O.; Mushtaq, M. A.; Tahir, M.; Yasin, G.; Liu, D.; Peng, Z.; Cai, X., Room Temperature Synthesis of Vertically Aligned Amorphous Ultrathin NiCo-LDH Nanosheets Bifunctional Flexible Supercapacitor Electrodes. Energy Environ. Mater. 2023, 7, e12545.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of nickel cobalt sulfide Nanosheet arrays on carbon cloth for performance-enhanced supercapacitor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, T.; Liu, J.; Zhang, L.; Cheng, B.; Yu, J., Construction of Nickel Cobalt Sulfide Nanosheet Arrays on Carbon Cloth for Performance-enhanced Supercapacitor. J. Mater. Sci. Technol. 2020, 47, 113-121.
